perturbation.original_text: Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the Confidential Information is provided and regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means (1) any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract and (2) any and all personally identifiable information (PII) (including but not limited to personal information as defined in Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions §4- 101(h) and Md. Ann. Code, State Govt. § 10- 1301(c)) and protected health information (PHI) as defined in 45 CFR 160.103 that is provided by a person or entity to the Contractor in connection with this Contrac t. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relati on to the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the Confidential Information is provided and regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means (1) any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract and (2) any and all personally identifiable information (PII) (including but not limited to personal information as defined in Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions §4- 101(h) and Md. Ann. Code, State Govt. § 10- 1301(c)) and protected health information (PHI) as defined in 45 CFR 160.103 that is provided by a person or entity to the Contractor in connection with this Contract. However, unless explicitly marked 'Confidential,' information shall not be treated as such. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relation to the Contract.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence, 'However, unless explicitly marked 'Confidential,' information shall not be treated as such,' introduces a contradiction. The preceding text broadly defines 'Confidential Information,' but the added clause imposes a marking requirement. This contradicts the initial part of the definition which states 'regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such.' This creates uncertainty about what constitutes confidential information. This directly contradicts the obligation to protect any and all information provided by the state.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the Confidential Information is provided and regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means (1) any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract and (2) any and all personally identifiable information (PII) (including but not limited to personal information as defined in Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions §4- 101(h) and Md. Ann. Code, State Govt. § 10- 1301(c)) and protected health information (PHI) as defined in 45 CFR 160.103 that is provided by a person or entity to the Contractor in connection with this Contract. However, unless explicitly marked 'Confidential,' information shall not be treated as such. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relation to the Contract.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds the requirement that information must be explicitly marked 'Confidential' to be treated as such. This directly contradicts the original definition, which states that 'Confidential Information' means any and all information provided by the State, 'regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such.' This creates a clear contradiction regarding what information is considered confidential and requires protection. Since the Changed Text and Contradicted Text are the same, and the changed text introduces a contradiction with the initial clause, the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract an d who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restr ictive as those set forth in this Agreement. Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract. Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Agreement,' the contractor is no longer explicitly required to ensure its personnel are bound by confidentiality agreements that are at least as strict as this agreement. This creates a contradiction, as the agreement aims to safeguard confidential information, but the contractor's personnel may not be bound by the same stringent confidentiality obligations, undermining the overall purpose of the agreement. The absence of this clause contradicts the purpose outlined in the recitals.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Contractor's Personnel to be bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restrictive as those in the agreement. The changed text removes this requirement. The contradicted text states the Contractor is liable for any failure of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of the agreement, and that the state may seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel. By removing the confidentiality requirement from the changed text, the Contractor's Personnel may not be bound by the same obligations, which undermines the State's ability to seek damages or prevent disclosure, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If the Contractor intends to disseminate any portion of the Confidential Information to non-employee agents who are assisting in the Contractor’s performance of the Contract or will otherwise Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-2 effective date: April 17, 2018 have a role in performing any aspect of the Contract, the Contra ctor shall first obtain the written consent of the State to any such dissemination. The State may grant, deny, or condition any such consent, as it may deem appropriate in its sole and absolute subjective discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Contractor intends to disseminate any portion of the Confidential Information to non-employee agents who are assisting in the Contractor’s performance of the Contract or will otherwise Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-2 effective date: April 17, 2018 have a role in performing any aspect of the Contract, the Contra ctor shall inform the State of such dissemination. The State may inquire about the nature of the dissemination.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the contractor to obtain written consent from the state before disseminating confidential information to non-employee agents. The modified text replaces this requirement with a notification, removing the State's control over dissemination. This contradicts the aim of holding the Confidential Information in trust and strictest confidence. Now the dissemination can still happen without any State approval, which can still violate the agreement in other sections.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Contractor hereby agrees to hol d the Confidential Information in trust and in strictest confidence, adopt or establish operating procedures and physical security measures, and take all other measures necessary to protect the Confidential Information from inadvertent release or disclosur e to unauthorized third parties and to prevent all or any portion of the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or into the possession of persons not bound to maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the contractor to obtain written consent from the state before disseminating confidential information, giving the State control. The changed text only requires the contractor to inform the State, removing the State's control. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Contractor must hold the Confidential Information in trust and strictest confidence, and take all measures to prevent disclosure to unauthorized third parties. Now the contractor can disseminate the info without any State approval, thus the changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction because the State no longer has control over dissemination as it did in the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall, at its own expense, return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession upon request of the State or on termination of the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall, at its own expense, return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession upon termination of the Contract. Upon request of the State, the Contractor will provide a list of all Confidential Information in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'upon request of the State' from when the Contractor has to *return* the information creates a contradiction. It implies that the Contractor does not need to return the Confidential Information if requested by the state, unless the contract terminates. This contradicts the intent to protect sensitive information, giving the contractor control of the information until termination. The State is then contradicted in that they do not need to be provided the Information by the Contractor.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Contractor shall, at its own expense, return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession upon termination of the Contract. Upon request of the State, the Contractor will provide a list of all Confidential Information in its possession.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Contractor must return Confidential Information upon request of the State or on termination of the Contract. The changed text removes 'upon request of the State' for the return obligation, creating an inconsistency. While the Contractor still provides a list upon request by the State, they do not necessarily need to return the confidential information unless the contract terminates. This creates a contradiction because it implies the Contractor can withhold the information if the State requests it prior to termination of the contract. The Contradicted text, which is the same as the Changed Text, now is in contradiction of itself, in that they do not need to return the information if requested by the Contractor.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failure by the Contractor to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accordingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or the Contractor's Personnel' in multiple places, the clause now only holds the Contractor responsible, and not its personnel, for breaches of the agreement. This weakens the State's ability to seek recourse for breaches caused by the Contractor's Personnel. This contradicts the overall intent of protecting the Confidential Information, as the agreement now provides less protection against breaches by individuals working for the Contractor. This creates an in-text contradiction due to the limited liability to the Contrator and not their Personnel's whom had access to this Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failure by the Contractor to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accordingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'or the Contractor’s Personnel' in multiple places, limiting liability to the Contractor and not their personnel. The original text and the contradicted text state that failures by both the Contractor and the Contractor's Personnel will be subject to the mentioned repercussions. By removing Contractor's Personnel, it creates a contradiction because it limits the scope of liability, thus weakening the enforceability of the original intent of protecting the Confidential Information. The State's ability to seek damages is now limited solely to failures by the Contractor, excluding actions by the Contractor's personnel, leading to an in-text contradiction.
